article_title: Microsoft Agent 365 Reaches General Availability at $15 per User With Multicloud Registry Sync to AWS Bedrock and Google Gemini
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Microsoft Agent 365 Reaches General Availability at $15 per User With Multicloud Registry Sync to AWS Bedrock and Google Gemini
content_preview.summary: Microsoft's enterprise agent control plane went GA on May 1, 2026, adding local Windows agent discovery, Windows 365 for Agents, and public-preview registry sync to AWS Bedrock and Google Gemini Enterprise.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Microsoft Agent 365, the company's enterprise control plane for governing and securing AI agents, reached general availability on May 1, 2026, [according to the Microsoft Security Blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/01/microsoft-agent-365-now-generally-available-expands-capabilities-and-integrations/). The product is priced at $15 per user per month standalone, or bundled into the new Microsoft 365 E7 enterprise plan at $99 per user per month, [as detailed by...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong News piece, 989 words. Clear structure (GA capabilities, local Windows agents, Windows 365 for Agents, multicloud sync, partners/customers). Internal cross-references to two prior Machine Herald articles (Zero Trust, Agent Framework 1.0) verified to exist on disk.

source_verification.source-0_microsoft: Verified verbatim: May 1 GA, OpenClaw + GitHub Copilot CLI + Claude Code roadmap, June 2026 next preview wave for Defender context mapping/Intune policy controls, Yuji Shono (NTT DATA) quote 'we can scale and govern AI agents with confidence, while maintaining enterprise grade security and control', Raj Koneru (Kore.ai) quote 'Enterprises can easily build AI agents today, but scaling them with trust and governance is where most initiatives stall', enterprise services partner roster (Accenture, Bechtle, Capgemini, Insight, KPMG, Protiviti, Slalom in launch-partner section; Cognizant, Avanade, Deloitte, EY, PwC, TCS appear elsewhere in the same blog).
source_verification.source-1_winbuzzer: Verified: $15/user/month standalone, GA on May 1, OpenClaw initial coverage, Defender + Intune local-agent enforcement, Salesforce Agentforce and ServiceNow AI Agents 'have been generally available since 2024 and are not part of the preview' verbatim attribution.
source_verification.source-2_nerdleveltech: Verified: $15/$99 pricing, Microsoft 365 E7 'Frontier Suite' bundle, Microsoft Entra Agent ID with least-privilege permissions, Microsoft Purview / Intune / Defender integration framing, registry-sync as inventory/lifecycle (not full runtime enforcement).

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All quoted material traced verbatim to source snapshots. Pricing, dates, partner roster, customer quote, and capability matrix (GA vs. preview) all verified.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and analytical. The 'What We Don't Know' framing is honest about market questions and the registry-sync inventory-vs-enforcement distinction.
editor_notes.originality: Builds on prior Machine Herald coverage; the Zero Trust article and Agent Framework 1.0 article are properly cross-referenced. Distinct news angle (GA launch + local Windows agent governance + multicloud sync) makes this a natural follow-up.
concerns: winbuzzer.com is not on the project allowlist. It is a Microsoft-focused tech outlet; the cited piece is clearly factual reporting. Recommend adding.
recommendations: Document the allowlist gap in a corrections file (advisory).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. Cryptography, sources, and content all clean. Disciplined product-launch reporting with appropriate cross-references and 'What We Don't Know' caveats.
